panels.description: How many records per second each topic is ingesting. Each topic represents a different format/compression/template combination.
panels.title: Message Rate (records/sec per topic)

panels.description: Bytes per second ingested by the broker for each topic. Shows raw data throughput including serialization overhead.
panels.title: Byte Throughput In (per topic)

panels.description: Average bytes stored per message on the broker. Lower values mean better compression. Compare this across topics to see how compression algorithms and message formats affect storage efficiency.
panels.title: Avg Bytes per Message (lower = better compression)

panels.description: Average bytes sent to the broker per produce request (batch). Larger batches mean the producer is packing more data per request, which improves compression ratios and reduces per-request overhead.
panels.title: Batch Size (bytes per produce request)

panels.description: Total bytes stored on disk for each topic across all brokers and partitions. Shows the cumulative storage impact of different format/compression choices.
panels.title: Topic Storage Size (bytes on disk)

panels.description: How many messages the producer packs into each produce request. Higher values indicate more efficient batching, which improves compression ratios and throughput.
panels.title: Messages per Produce Request (batching efficiency)

panels.description: End-to-end time the broker takes to process a produce request (p50, p95, p99). Includes queuing, disk writes, replication, and response. Higher values during a test indicate the broker is under more stress from that format/compression combo.
panels.title: Produce Request Latency (p50 / p95 / p99)

panels.description: End-to-end time the broker takes to serve a fetch (consumer read) request. Shows how message format and compression affect read performance.
panels.title: Fetch Request Latency (p50 / p95 / p99)

panels.description: Breakdown of where time is spent during produce requests (p99). Queue = waiting for a request handler. Local = disk I/O and CPU. Remote = waiting for follower replicas to acknowledge. Response = sending the response back to the producer.
panels.title: Produce Latency Breakdown at p99 (stacked)

panels.description: How much headroom the broker has. Request Handler threads process requests (disk, CPU); Network Processor threads handle socket I/O. Values below 40% idle indicate the broker is under heavy load. Near 0% means the broker is saturated.
panels.title: Broker Idle % (higher = more headroom)

panels.description: Number of partitions where one or more replicas are not in sync. Should always be 0 in a healthy cluster. Non-zero values during a test mean the workload is stressing the cluster beyond its capacity.
panels.title: Under-Replicated Partitions

panels.description: Number of active controllers in the cluster. Must always be exactly 1. If 0, the cluster has no leader. If >1, there is a split-brain.
panels.title: Active Controllers

panels.description: Number of requests waiting in the broker's request queue. High values indicate the broker cannot keep up with the incoming workload.
panels.title: Request Queue Size

panels.description: Offline partitions should always be 0. Non-zero means data is unavailable.
panels.title: Offline Partitions

panels.description: CPU cores used by each broker process. Shows the CPU cost of compression and serialization.
panels.title: CPU Usage (per broker)

panels.description: Total JVM memory (heap + non-heap) used by each broker. Shows the memory cost of different workloads.
panels.title: JVM Memory Used (per broker)

panels.description: Fraction of time each broker spends in garbage collection. Compression-heavy workloads can increase GC pressure due to buffer allocations.
panels.title: GC Time (per broker)
